title: Object-Oriented Programming
simple_definition: OOP is a way of organizing code around 'objects' — things that have properties (data) and behaviors (actions). Like a blueprint for creating things.
technical_definition: OOP is a programming paradigm based on classes and objects. Python supports the four pillars: Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction.
analogy: Think of a class as a blueprint for a house. The blueprint defines rooms, doors, windows (attributes) and what you can do in each room (methods). Each actual house built from that blueprint is an object (instance).
example_desc: A complete OOP example with classes, inheritance, and polymorphism.
output:
Rex says Woof
Whiskers purrs... Meow
Rex knows: ['sit', 'shake']
Rex says Woof
Whiskers purrs... Meow
Buddy says Woof
logic_steps: __init__ is the constructor — called automatically when you create an object
self refers to the current instance of the class
Instance attributes (self.x) belong to each object; class attributes are shared
Inheritance: Child class gets all methods/attributes of Parent class
super().__init__() calls the parent class constructor
Polymorphism: same method name, different behavior in different classes
Python uses MRO (Method Resolution Order) to determine which method to call
edge_cases: Forgetting 'self' as first parameter in methods causes TypeError
Multiple inheritance can cause diamond problem — Python uses C3 linearization (MRO)
__str__ vs __repr__: str() calls __str__, repr() calls __repr__ for debugging
common_mistakes: Confusing class attributes and instance attributes — class attributes are shared by all instances
Not calling super().__init__() in child class — parent attributes won't be initialized
Using mutable class attributes (like lists) — they are shared across all instances!
interview_tips: Know all 4 pillars: Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction
@property decorator creates getter/setter without explicit method calls
Abstract classes use abc module: from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

easy.question: Create a class 'Rectangle' with width, height and a method to calculate area
Create a class 'BankAccount' with deposit and withdraw methods
easy.hint: Track balance as instance attribute, check for sufficient funds

easy.question: Create a class 'Student' with name, grade and a method to check if passing

medium.question: Create a class hierarchy: Shape -> Circle, Rectangle, Triangle with area()
medium.hint: Use inheritance, override area() in each subclass

medium.question: Implement a Stack class using a list with push, pop, peek, is_empty

hard.question: Implement a linked list class with insert, delete, and search methods
hard.hint: Create a Node class first, then LinkedList class with head pointer

hard.question: Create a decorator class that counts how many times a function is called

subtopics.title: Classes & Objects
subtopics.explanation: A class is a blueprint. An object is an instance created from that blueprint. __init__ is the constructor that runs when you create an object.
subtopics.code:
class Dog:
    species = 'Canis lupus'  # class attribute
    
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name  # instance attribute
        self.age = age
    
    def bark(self):
        return f'{self.name} says: Woof!'
    
    def info(self):
        return f'{self.name} ({self.age} yrs) - {self.species}'

# Create objects
dog1 = Dog('Rex', 3)
dog2 = Dog('Buddy', 5)

print(dog1.bark())
print(dog2.info())
print(dog1.species)  # class attribute
print(Dog.species)   # same thing

# Modify instance attribute
dog1.age = 4
print(dog1.age)  # 4
print(dog2.age)  # 5 (unchanged)
subtopics.output:
Rex says: Woof!
Buddy (5 yrs) - Canis lupus
Canis lupus
Canis lupus
4
5
subtopics.tip: self refers to the current instance. Always use self.attribute to store instance data.

subtopics.title: Inheritance
subtopics.explanation: Inheritance lets a child class reuse code from a parent class. Use super() to call the parent's methods.
subtopics.output:
Rex barks: Woof!
Whiskers meows: Meow!
Rex fetches the ball!
True
True
False
subtopics.tip: Always call super().__init__() in child class to properly initialize parent attributes.

subtopics.title: Encapsulation
subtopics.explanation: Encapsulation hides internal data and provides controlled access through methods. Use _ for protected, __ for private.
subtopics.tip: __ (double underscore) makes attributes private via name mangling. Use @property for clean getter/setter access.

subtopics.title: Polymorphism
subtopics.explanation: Polymorphism means the same method name works differently for different classes. One interface, many implementations.
subtopics.output:
Circle: area=78.54
Rectangle: area=24.00
Triangle: area=12.00
5
3
1
subtopics.tip: Polymorphism is the power of OOP. Write code that works with any object that has the right method — don't check types!

subtopics.title: Dunder Methods
subtopics.explanation: Dunder (double underscore) methods like __str__, __len__, __add__ let your objects work with Python's built-in operations.
subtopics.syntax:
class MyClass:
    def __init__(self): pass
    def __str__(self): return 'string repr'
    def __repr__(self): return 'debug repr'
    def __len__(self): return 0
    def __add__(self, other): pass
    def __eq__(self, other): pass
subtopics.tip: Dunder methods make your objects feel native to Python. __str__ for print(), __len__ for len(), __add__ for + operator.

problem_first:
Suppose you are building a school management system.

Without OOP:
student1_name = 'Alice'
student1_age = 20
student1_grade = 'A'

student2_name = 'Bob'
student2_age = 21
student2_grade = 'B'

# 100 students = 300 variables! 😱

With OOP:
class Student:
    def __init__(self, name, age, grade):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age
        self.grade = grade

s1 = Student('Alice', 20, 'A')
s2 = Student('Bob', 21, 'B')
# 100 students = 100 objects, same class!
why_matters:
OOP is used in EVERY large application:
🌐🌐 Web frameworks: Django, Flask use classes everywhere
🎮🎮 Games: Player, Enemy, Weapon are all classes
🤖🤖 ML: sklearn models are classes (fit, predict methods)
🏦 Banking: Account, Transaction, Customer classes
📱 GUI: Button, Window, Form are all objects

Without OOP, large programs become impossible to manage.
important_points: ⭐ Class is a BLUEPRINT; Object is an INSTANCE created from that blueprint
⭐ __init__ is the constructor — runs automatically when you create an object
⭐ self refers to the current instance — always first parameter in methods
⭐ Class attributes are SHARED by all instances; instance attributes are UNIQUE per object
⭐ Inheritance: child class gets ALL methods/attributes of parent class
⭐ super().__init__() calls parent constructor — always call it in child __init__
⭐ Polymorphism: same method name, different behavior in different classes
⭐ Encapsulation: __ (double underscore) makes attributes private via name mangling

steps.action: return f'{self.name} says: Woof!' → 'Rex says: Woof!'
